participants.extracted: Generally healthy adult smokers aged 18 to 75 years who were motivated to quit smoking and were enrolled in two identical randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials conducted between June 2003 and April 2005. Participants with any history of prior bupropion or varenicline exposure were excluded. The pooled sample included 2,052 randomized participants: 696 assigned to varenicline, 671 to bupropion SR, and 685 to placebo. All groups received brief cessation counseling and a self-help booklet, with smoking status followed during treatment and through 52 weeks.

interventions.extracted: Participants in each study were randomized at baseline to receive varenicline, bupropion, or placebo for 12 weeks. All participants received a self-help booklet on smoking cessation (Clearing the Air: How to Quit Smoking) at baseline. The target quit day occurred after the first week of drug treatment and was day 8 (week 1 visit). Although the trials were placebo-controlled for drug assignment, all arms, including placebo, also received brief cessation counseling of up to 10 minutes at baseline and at clinic visits during weeks 1 to 13, 24, 36, 44, and 52. Brief 5-minute telephone counseling calls were scheduled for day 3 after the target quit day and at weeks 16, 20, 28, 32, 40, and 48 during non-treatment follow-up.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure smoking abstinence outcomes as the primary and secondary efficacy endpoints: continuous abstinence (defined as not even a puff, biochemically confirmed with expired carbon monoxide ≤10 ppm) for weeks 9–12 as the primary endpoint, and continuous abstinence for weeks 9–52 as the secondary endpoint. In the post hoc analysis, quitting pattern was also assessed by classifying successful quitters into immediate quitters (quit on the target quit day and remained continuously abstinent through weeks 2–12) and delayed quitters (first quit after the target quit day or had early lapses but later achieved continuous abstinence through weeks 9–12). Continuous abstinence was evaluated weekly during treatment for weeks 2–12 and during follow-up at weeks 13, 24, 36, 44, and 52. The systematic review outcomes of interest for this study also include adverse events and safety-related outcomes such as abnormal dreams, depression, insomnia, nausea, serious adverse events, and suicidal ideation, although these were not described as the main endpoints in the provided trial text.
